content:
Ryan, I’d do two things at once: teach my child how to read the Bible well, and keep walking in charity with my elder.

- During Psalm 19: I’d lean over to my 10-year-old and whisper, “This is a song. Songs use pictures to tell true things. When it says the sun runs its course, that’s how it looks to us—it’s not giving a science lesson. The point is that the sky shouts, ‘God is glorious!’ and that God’s Word is perfect.” Afterward I might say, “Inerrancy means the Bible tells the truth in the way each part intends—poetry with images, narrative with history, wisdom with proverbs.”

- Later that day: I’d open Psalm 19 again and show the two halves—creation’s voice (verses 1–6) and Scripture’s voice (verses 7–11). Then I’d read Genesis 1 and point out how it reads as tightly structured narrative: “And God said… and it was so… and there was evening and morning, the nth day.” I’d show Exodus 20:11 too, where the Sabbath command is grounded in those six days. I’d explain, “Faithful Christians do differ on ‘how long’ the days are, but we don’t call history ‘poetry’ just to make people happy. We let the text set its own terms.”

- With my elder: I wouldn’t correct him publicly. I’d ask for a private meeting. I’d say, “I want unity, and I’m happy to distinguish primary from secondary issues. Could we teach our people how genre works without requiring everyone to read Genesis 1 as poetry? Unity comes from the truth we confess, not by flattening passages.” I’d ask if the church can state clear essentials (God created all things; creation is good; Adam and Eve real; we bear God’s image; sin and death enter through Adam; the Sabbath pattern) while allowing charitable differences on age and timing—without binding consciences to a “poetic only” reading.

- With my child again: I’d model humility. “We honor our leaders and pray for them. When Christians disagree on hard texts, we don’t fight—we study, pray, and speak the truth in love. The biggest ‘stumbling block’ in the Bible is the cross, and we will never remove that. But we also won’t twist Scripture to avoid offense.”

In short: I teach my 10-year-old that poetry can speak truly with pictures, that Genesis 1 is best read as real events told in elevated, orderly prose, and that unity is built by honesty, charity, and confidence in the Word—not by rebranding passages to keep the peace.
